title: Variables
summary: Name values and show whether reassignment is expected.
refresher: Use `const` when a binding stays fixed and `let` when it must be reassigned. Declare either name before its first use.

blocks.body: Use `const` when a binding stays fixed and `let` when it must be reassigned. Declare either name before its first use. Name values and show whether reassignment is expected.

A variable name is a label your code uses to reach a value. `const` means that label cannot be reassigned, while `let` permits reassignment. This rule concerns the label itself. A const object or array may still have its contents changed because the label continues to point to the same value.

blocks.caption: The course binding stays fixed while the numeric count changes.
Declare once, then reassign only when the design requires change.

blocks.body: After the corrected code runs, there is one variable named `count`. Its first value is one, and the assignment replaces that value with two. A block inside braces may declare its own name, but that separate scope should be intentional.

blocks.title: Watch this detail
blocks.body: Repeating `let score` in one block redeclares the name and causes a syntax error; reassignment omits `let`.

blocks.prompt: Which declaration suits a changing counter?
blocks.why: A let binding may be reassigned as the counter changes.
Compound assignment adds three and stores seven in the existing variable.
